flashcards.q: What is work in scientific terms?
flashcards.a: Work is the product of force and displacement in the direction of force (W = F × s). Requires both force AND displacement to be non-zero.

flashcards.q: What are the two conditions necessary for work to be done?
flashcards.a: Condition 1: A force must act on the object. Condition 2: The object must be displaced in the direction of the force. Both must be satisfied for work to occur.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of work?
flashcards.a: Newton-metre (N·m) or joule (J). 1 joule = 1 newton × 1 metre. It is the work done when a 1 N force displaces an object by 1 m.

flashcards.q: Define 1 joule.
flashcards.a: 1 joule is the amount of work done on an object when a force of 1 newton displaces it by 1 metre in the direction of the force (1 J = 1 N·m).

flashcards.q: What does it mean when work is positive?
flashcards.a: Work is positive when force and displacement are in the same direction. Positive work increases an object's energy or speed.

flashcards.q: What does it mean when work is negative?
flashcards.a: Work is negative when force and displacement are in opposite directions. Negative work decreases an object's energy or speed. Example: friction opposes motion, doing negative work.

flashcards.q: Does gravity do positive or negative work on a rising object?
flashcards.a: Negative work. Gravity acts downward while displacement is upward (opposite directions). When the object falls, gravity does positive work.

flashcards.q: Can work be zero even if force acts on an object?
flashcards.a: Yes. If there is no displacement (s = 0), then W = F × 0 = 0, regardless of force magnitude. Pushing a stationary wall does zero work despite great effort.

flashcards.q: Define energy.
flashcards.a: Energy is the capacity of an object or system to do work. An object with energy can exert forces and cause displacements. Unit: joule (J), same as work.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of energy?
flashcards.a: Joule (J). Larger unit: kilojoule (kJ), where 1 kJ = 1000 J. Energy has identical units to work because energy is measured as capacity to perform work.

flashcards.q: What is kinetic energy?
flashcards.a: The energy possessed by an object due to its motion. A moving object can do work: a bullet penetrates targets, wind turns windmills, flowing water drives turbines.

flashcards.q: Write the formula for kinetic energy.
flashcards.a: E_k = (1/2) × m × v², where m is mass (kg) and v is velocity (m/s). Kinetic energy is directly proportional to mass but proportional to the SQUARE of velocity.

flashcards.q: How does kinetic energy depend on velocity?
flashcards.a: Kinetic energy is proportional to v² (velocity squared). Doubling velocity quadruples kinetic energy. This quadratic relationship makes speed critical in determining collision damage.

flashcards.q: If an object's velocity increases from v to 2v, how does kinetic energy change?
flashcards.a: Kinetic energy increases 4 times. E_k(2v) = (1/2)m(2v)² = 4 × (1/2)mv² = 4 × E_k(v). This shows why high-speed collisions cause much more damage.

flashcards.q: What is potential energy?
flashcards.a: The energy possessed by an object due to its position or configuration (shape). It is 'potential' because it has potential to do work but isn't currently doing so.

flashcards.q: Write the formula for gravitational potential energy.
flashcards.a: E_p = m × g × h, where m is mass (kg), g is gravitational acceleration (≈10 m/s²), and h is height above reference level (m).

flashcards.q: What does gravitational potential energy depend on?
flashcards.a: Gravitational potential energy is directly proportional to mass, gravity, and height. E_p = mgh. Double any factor, and potential energy doubles.

flashcards.q: Does potential energy depend on the reference level chosen?
flashcards.a: Yes. An object at a given height has different potential energies relative to different reference levels. However, CHANGES in potential energy are independent of reference level and physically meaningful.

flashcards.q: What is mechanical energy?
flashcards.a: The sum of kinetic energy and potential energy of an object. E_mechanical = E_k + E_p. In isolated systems without dissipative forces, mechanical energy is conserved.

flashcards.q: State the Law of Conservation of Energy.
flashcards.a: Energy can be transformed from one form to another, but the total energy in an isolated system remains constant. Energy is neither created nor destroyed; it only changes form.

flashcards.q: Does the Law of Conservation mean mechanical energy (E_k + E_p) never decreases?
flashcards.a: No. Mechanical energy decreases due to friction converting it to heat. However, total energy (including heat) remains conserved. Mechanical energy appears to decrease because it transforms to less useful forms.

flashcards.q: Name forms of energy that can be transformed to other forms.
flashcards.a: Kinetic energy, potential energy, chemical energy, heat energy, electrical energy, light energy, nuclear energy. All can be transformed, and total energy is conserved.

flashcards.q: Give an example of energy transformation.
flashcards.a: A waterfall: gravitational potential energy (water at height) → kinetic energy (falling water) → mechanical energy (turbine rotation) → electrical energy. Continuous transformation with total energy conserved.

flashcards.q: Define power.
flashcards.a: The rate of doing work or the rate of energy transfer. It measures how fast work is done or energy is transferred.

flashcards.q: Write the formula for power.
flashcards.a: P = W / t, where P is power (watts), W is work (joules), and t is time (seconds). Power equals work divided by time.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of power?
flashcards.a: Watt (W). 1 watt = 1 joule per second (1 W = 1 J/s). Larger unit: kilowatt (kW), where 1 kW = 1000 W.

flashcards.q: Define 1 watt.
flashcards.a: 1 watt is the power when 1 joule of work is done in 1 second. A 1 W device transfers 1 J of energy per second.

flashcards.q: Define average power.
flashcards.a: Average Power = Total work done / Total time taken. Used when power varies over time. For constant power, average power equals instantaneous power.

flashcards.q: Do two people doing the same work at different speeds have the same power?
flashcards.a: No. Same work done faster requires higher power. Power is inversely proportional to time for constant work: P = W/t. Less time means more power.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between power and time for constant work?
flashcards.a: P ∝ 1/t (Power is inversely proportional to time). Doing work twice as fast requires twice the power. Doing work twice as slow requires half the power.

flashcards.q: Can power be expressed as P = F × v?
flashcards.a: Yes. Since W = F × s and P = W/t = (F × s)/t = F × (s/t) = F × v. Power can be expressed as force multiplied by velocity. Useful for analyzing moving objects.

flashcards.q: For lifting objects, what is the power formula?
flashcards.a: P = mgh / t = W / t, where mgh is work done against gravity. For raising an object of mass m through height h in time t, power = mgh/t.

flashcards.q: What is the Work-Energy Theorem?
flashcards.a: The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy. W_net = ΔE_k = Final KE - Initial KE. It connects work and motion without needing force details.

flashcards.q: Write the Work-Energy Theorem as an equation.
flashcards.a: W_net = (1/2)m(v_f)² - (1/2)m(v_i)², where v_f is final velocity and v_i is initial velocity. Net work equals the change in kinetic energy.

flashcards.q: If net work on an object is positive, what happens to kinetic energy?
flashcards.a: Kinetic energy increases. The object's speed increases. Positive work adds energy to the system, accelerating the object.

flashcards.q: If net work on an object is negative, what happens to kinetic energy?
flashcards.a: Kinetic energy decreases. The object's speed decreases. Negative work removes energy from the system, decelerating the object. Example: friction doing negative work.

flashcards.q: Can an object have zero kinetic energy while experiencing forces?
flashcards.a: Yes. At the highest point of upward motion, kinetic energy is zero (v = 0) but gravity still acts. Zero kinetic energy means zero speed instantaneously, not zero acceleration or forces.

flashcards.q: How do kinetic energy and potential energy transform during free fall?
flashcards.a: As object falls, potential energy decreases while kinetic energy increases proportionally. Total mechanical energy remains constant. PE → KE continuous transformation.

flashcards.q: At the highest point of a ball's flight, what is its kinetic energy?
flashcards.a: Zero. At maximum height, velocity becomes zero instantaneously, so E_k = (1/2)mv² = 0. All initial kinetic energy converted to potential energy.

flashcards.q: True or False: Holding a heavy load stationary involves work.
flashcards.a: False. Although force is applied and energy is expended (metabolically), there is no displacement. Work = F × s = F × 0 = 0 scientifically. Metabolic work ≠ mechanical work.

flashcards.q: Does gravity do work on a horizontal motion?
flashcards.a: No. Gravity acts vertically downward; horizontal motion is perpendicular to gravity. Work requires force component in direction of displacement. Perpendicular force does zero work.

flashcards.q: What is elastic potential energy?
flashcards.a: Energy stored in stretched or compressed elastic objects (springs, rubber bands) due to change in shape. Released when object returns to original shape.

flashcards.q: How is elastic potential energy similar to gravitational potential energy?
flashcards.a: Both are potential energy stored due to configuration (height for gravity, deformation for elastic). Both can be converted to kinetic energy. Both depend on how far from natural state the object is.

flashcards.q: Does the path taken matter for calculating work done by gravity?
flashcards.a: No. Work by gravity depends only on vertical height change, not path. Climbing stairs or taking an incline at same height change results in same gravitational work.

flashcards.q: True or False: Kinetic energy can be negative.
flashcards.a: False. E_k = (1/2)mv² is always non-negative (v² is always positive). Kinetic energy cannot be negative. Velocity can be negative (opposite direction), but v² is always positive.

flashcards.q: True or False: Potential energy can be negative.
flashcards.a: True. If reference level is chosen above the object, E_p = mgh is negative. However, changes in potential energy are more physically meaningful than absolute values.

flashcards.q: What happens to mechanical energy in a system with friction?
flashcards.a: Mechanical energy decreases. Friction does negative work, removing mechanical energy. This energy converts to heat. Total energy (mechanical + heat) remains conserved.

flashcards.q: Is energy lost in collisions where objects stick together?
flashcards.a: Mechanical energy is 'lost' (becomes heat, sound, deformation). However, total energy is conserved—it just transforms to less useful forms. Momentum is conserved in such collisions.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between work and energy transfer?
flashcards.a: When work is done on an object, energy is transferred to it. Positive work increases energy; negative work decreases energy. Work is the mechanism of energy transfer.

flashcards.q: Can power be calculated from energy and time?
flashcards.a: Yes. Power = Energy / Time = E/t. Any form of energy transfer can be analyzed using this power formula.

flashcards.q: How does air resistance affect mechanical energy conservation?
flashcards.a: Air resistance does negative work, removing mechanical energy from the system. This energy converts to heat. Total energy is conserved; mechanical energy decreases while thermal energy increases.

flashcards.q: What is the unit of energy per unit time?
flashcards.a: Watt (W) or joule per second (J/s). This is power. Energy rate = Power. 1 kilowatt-hour (kWh) is energy consumed at 1 kW power for 1 hour.

flashcards.q: True or False: Two objects of same mass and same kinetic energy must have same velocity.
flashcards.a: True. E_k = (1/2)mv² is the same, and m is the same, so v² is the same. Same mass and kinetic energy implies same speed. Direction may differ.

flashcards.q: How does doubling the mass affect kinetic energy for same velocity?
flashcards.a: Kinetic energy doubles. E_k ∝ m (linear relationship with mass). Double mass → double kinetic energy at same velocity.

flashcards.q: How does doubling the height affect gravitational potential energy?
flashcards.a: Potential energy doubles. E_p ∝ h (linear relationship with height). Double height → double potential energy.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between work and change in kinetic energy?
flashcards.a: They are equal. W_net = ΔE_k. Net work done always equals kinetic energy change. This is the Work-Energy Theorem.

flashcards.q: Can mechanical energy be converted to non-mechanical forms?
flashcards.a: Yes. Friction converts mechanical energy to heat. Deformation converts mechanical to elastic and heat energy. Light, sound, electromagnetic energy are products of mechanical energy conversion.

flashcards.q: True or False: Power can be constant even if force and velocity change.
flashcards.a: True. P = F × v. If force and velocity change such that their product remains constant, power is constant. Example: car engine at constant power with varying force and speed.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between speed and kinetic energy?
flashcards.a: Speed is magnitude of velocity (scalar). Kinetic energy depends on v² (quadratic), not v (linear). Kinetic energy = 4 × original when speed doubles, but speed itself only doubles.

flashcards.q: True or False: An object moving faster always does more work.
flashcards.a: False. Work depends on force and displacement, not velocity directly. Fast-moving objects have more kinetic energy, but work done depends on forces applied and distances traveled.

flashcards.q: Define reference level in potential energy.
flashcards.a: Reference level is an arbitrary chosen position where potential energy is zero. Common choice: ground level for gravity. Potential energy at height h is measured relative to this reference.

flashcards.q: How is work related to change in potential energy?
flashcards.a: When work is done against a conservative force (like gravity), it increases potential energy. Work done by gravity equals negative of change in potential energy: W_gravity = -ΔE_p.

flashcards.q: True or False: All forms of energy can be converted to any other form.
flashcards.a: Mostly true. Energy transformations are possible (kinetic ↔ potential, chemical → electrical, heat → mechanical via engines). However, not all transformations are practical or efficient.
